speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This conversation with Helmer Ringsage took place at his home in Moscow on February 12th, 1976. The interviewer is Sam Schrager.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: My, uh, I was the oldest, and I'll tell you the words my mother told me when she was on her dying bed. I was just old enough to realize that she was dying, you know.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And them days they claimed, I don't know why they did, but they kind of had the idea that the older one had to look after younger ones.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Well, all the rest of them were the youngest. I was the oldest. And when she just, just before she took her last breath, she says, Helmer, she says, you're the oldest, you look after the children.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And then she died, just that way. Boy, I never forgot it either. And so I did, I tried, you know, to help my, well, here's what happened. After mother died, see, well that Simon, the one that died with cancer, I'm telling you about, he was only a kid, seven years old. No, wait a minute, see now. No, he wasn't that old then, but I mean when he was cooking.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: He was seven. No, he was only about four when she died. But he was cooking when he was seven. Well, later on, let's see, when he was seven, that would be about, he was born in, just a minute, he's seven years younger than I am, that would be 95 when he was born. Seven, now we figured out when he was seven, that would be 95. She died in 1901, now wait a minute. So he would have been six. He was six years old when she died. Well, I guess Dad learned him that year, so when he was seven, a year after that, he became the kind of the kitchen guy to look after things. Because we boys, our older boys, Steinert and I, when it come harvest time, I mean, we had to go out and do the shocking to them bunnles, see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was he, could he handle that at seven? Could he take care of the kitchen?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Well, he was a faithful guy.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Dad depended on him. He could direct him and he was a wonderful kid. But that younger, and then, no, that wasn't the youngest, that was Julius. He was a mischievous little devil. He was our radio in the home. And he, one time, that was before Dad, let's see, yeah, that was before Dad got that house built, see.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And we had, I'll show you the kitchen part, it's over there. He had a log house in this kitchen part on the back of that, and that's where Mother died in. Now, I had a cousin down here, of course, she lives here in Moscow.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Her and her brother and his wife and her husband come up here one day to visit. That's my cousin, see. And she, I happened to mention that my mother died when she was not quite 31, or 32. She's just past 31. She's a young woman. And then she foolishly asked, you know, made me mad right away.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: She says, why didn't she live any longer?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Well, I said, come here, I'll show you what I showed her.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: You see that little, Dad used that for chicken house, little wands, board thickness of a bedroom, and a wood stove to heat it with.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Now I said, I'd like to see you live in something like that and raise a family. That's what she, that last baby she had was born in that place.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And her jumping on to me like that, her brother sat in there, and her husband, no, I didn't give a damn about it. For her asking, for her talking like that, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was your mother sick for, was it a long sickness?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Oh, well, about a year. She had TB for about a whole year.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And oh boy, she'd cough sometimes, you don't choke. You'd think she was going to pass out most any time. She put in there dead. She'd choke and sit there and wheeze a long time before she'd get her breath, you know. Oh, she really suffered. But, and then to top it all, my wife got all sugar diabetes. But she was tough, she'd grade the family of four.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Here's my family.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: This is my wife.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And I worked in the woods.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And you see, I worked in the woods. This was taken in 1936, before any of them was married. This is my youngest daughter, she's the one who lives married, and she's got three daughters now. But there, she was eleven, and she was sixteen. Wait, no. Yeah, she was sixteen. And she was eighteen, and he was twenty. He was born in sixteen. And she was born in eighteen, and she was born in twenty.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And she wasn't born until nineteen twenty -five. She's five years younger than that.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And my grandma, my Mrs., was forty -four, and I was thirty -six. I took a good picture there. Well, we all did. We got the hell of a good picture. It really is a good one. Anyway, I had work. You're saying you worked in the woods. That year I'd been up in the woods, saw on with a crosscut, see. And I had three partners. I tell you that, but that's what's kept me going. I've had good health, strength, and I guess the good Lord's been on my side all the time. I've had close calls, putting in to get killed, taking one eye up, and not this stuff. Oh yeah, I didn't tell you that. Then my brother Simon, the one that died with cancer, that's the first year I worked in the woods, nineteen hundred and twelve. And I was twenty -four years old. And so we had roped together, I guess. He was on the farm with Dad, but he was seventeen then.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: You see, I was seven years older. He was seventeen, I was twenty -four. But he's a big man then already.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Good, husky man, and we always did get along good. So he wanted to get to work too, off the farm, see, in the winter. Long in the fall it was, after harvest field work was done. So I said, okay, he come up, and the first job we got was sawing out in the woods, falling timber.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Well, it was both greenhorns, the first year, you see, we never knew a damn thing about it.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Well, of course, in them days they had what they call undercutter, to chop the undercut to fall your tree. And so,

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: my golly, well, and that's for a highland. I got a book, I'll show you that book, the camp I worked at. The kids, they bought it for me. I guess they located it somewhere. It's Bovill, close to the picture of Bovill, see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Which camp was that?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Well, that was Camp Eight. Oh, I know about that one.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That was right near Collins there, right?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Well, it was about, I don't remember, it was kind of south east of Bovill.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah, okay, so, but you had to cut this tree down?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Yeah, we had to cut, I was going to tell you about that. I was cutting these, he and Simon and I was following this timber, see. And, well, this undercutter couldn't keep us going, because we was cutting them, you cut them forty foot logs, and one of them swedes, they filed them saws good, and there's a white pine to saw, and god, we just about walked the tailbone off that poor guy. So, all he had to do was undercut and mark the log. We didn't have to mark, we just went along and cut them off, see.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: God damn, we shouldn't have done it. We only got two and a half a day. Six bits for gold a day. But, dammit, we were just interacting, and we enjoyed it. And, we was getting good feeding camp, you know, Potlatch Lumber Company fed good, so that we was full of pistol pittagers, you call it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you put in a ten hour day then?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Ten hours, boy! For two and a half, two bits a day, two bits an hour. And so, in a way, then, well, then it got, so, well, I, here's the deal. I applied for a job at the landing. I didn't want to stay out in the woods all winter, see. God damn snow would get pretty bad.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: So, I figured it'd be a little nicer down there, and I told my brother, Simon, he's a young kid, only seventeen, I said, Simon, I said, you better get a job in the kitchen, you're waiting on the tables for the lumberjacks. And, he wouldn't get so much a day, but he got so much a month, forty dollars.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I said, that'll be better for you. Yeah, okay, he agreed. And that's the way we worked it. We went down there to where there's a donkey, I'll show you that pretty soon. And, and, I was loading, I mean, no, I wasn't loading. They had a top loader, stand on the car, load, placing these logs. And there's two guys, them days, now they got one big grapple fork, or, tongs they call them, to load. Them days, it was just a crotch chain, with a hook, two men, one at each end of the log. Well, this first hoistman that we had at camp eight, he was darn good.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: He drank like a fish, but he was sober enough to do his work good. So, then, by golly, it went on and on and on.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you switch?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: It didn't long, fine and dandy, but Simon, my brother, you see, was in the kitchen doing good.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So, what was you doing?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Well, I was doing one, I was one of them that was doing the hooking.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Another guy, not Simon, my brother, he got into the kitchen, see. So, then I, that was fine, we got along fine with that guy, see.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And, pretty soon, Gil Pippen was our foreman. One -eyed, old, big, Irishman, I guess, and he weighed around 250. And, by God, he wanted to move us down to another setting, and that was just a young kid, 16 years old, doing the hoisting.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And, that son of a bitch, he'd get reckless, see, he'd bring that log up a ways, and then he'd jerk it. And, the cotch changed the fly ring to fly ever which wing.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Oh, God, we all three just decided we'd quit. Top loader and I and the other hooker. Dangerous. If we didn't get back to our original hoister.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: That'd be dangerous.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: God damn, they just put near got us. So, we got back to camp.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Old Gil, well, he said he couldn't fire that kid, he said his dad was a good cook. Well, that's what, he had a good, sounded like a good reason. Well, alright, we said we was gonna quit. By God, we did quit, we didn't fool, we quit.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And, I went there forever. Now, that's the story I wanted to get you, I'm gonna tell you. I got to Elk River, my brother still stayed in the kitchen.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I told him, I said, you better stay there, you're inside. And, I said, that's a good, fine place for you. But, I said, I'm going to Elk River, trying to get a job in the mill. See, they run the mill there at Elk River. And, sure enough, I got there and I got a job, alright.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: But, I didn't get what I wanted, I wanted to be a fireman.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: You know, them days they had, they didn't have electricity in Elk River.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: They had to use dynamos, and them dynamos was fed with this sawdust from these, them big boilers was fed with this sawdust, see, at weekends. Weekends, they had to fire them, I had to fire them when I got the job. I didn't, wait now. You wanted a job. I wanted that job, see, they said, well, wait till somebody gets drunk. In them days, they had a lot of guys getting drunk.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: They had good whiskey, you know.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And, they said, well, I tell you, if you'll take a job sweeping the floor down here with a jab, till we get a drunk guy, then by golly, we'll put you on.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And, sure enough, wasn't about a week, I guess, that I had to sweep that floor down there.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And, then I got this other job and they said, well, I said, well, I said, what am I going to do during the week?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Oh, I said, ten pump. And, that was, say, that's the time I had to work twelve hours, night shift, twelve from six to six.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And, by golly, and I couldn't afford, I only got three, see, how much is that?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Yeah, that's only two bits an hour, three dollars for that twelve hours.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How many days a week did you work?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Oh, well, I had to work every day.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Seven days a week?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Well, you see, because, well, not all day, no, I mean, I was firing them boilers Friday night, Saturday night, and Sunday night. Three nights.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Rest of the time, I had a ten pump. I didn't have no weekends on. But, I wasn't carried on satisfied, I was young, I was only twenty -four.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: The kid, well, then pretty soon my brother wrote me. He said, the cooks are getting drunk.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Said, I'll tell you, going, oh, he said, I don't like to stay here, he wrote and told me. Well, I felt sorry for him, I knew he wanted to work. And, I thought, by golly, I'll try and get a job out in the camp then.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: So, I did.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I got a job out, what they call, C, Camp C, out of Alp River. And, that was what they called, bullcaw, you cut logs and split up wood for the cook car and for the campers, I mean, loggers. And, by golly, you know, they give us wet wood, wet white pine, we worked our heads off. Well, it was up to two o 'clock in the morning trying to get that, get all them fires going. We had to get the heart of the wood that was any good at all for cooking with them. They burnt that in the cook stove scene. You had to keep the cooks going whether you got the bunk fires going or not. And, we used coal oil to start them, but, God, it was, you know what wet wood will do. Well, by God, we stayed there, I don't remember, about a week or two. And, we got fed up. I told the boss, I said, for God's sake, I just get somebody else. And, I told Simon, I said, you better go on home back to farm, I said.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: So, he'd come out with you to work on that. Yeah, he'd come over there, you see, and got on this job. Two of you was working. He was already on the job with me when we quit. And, I said, uh.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You were working, two of you worked together?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Yeah, yeah, we did. We had to saw that wood together, you see, just like we did when it was falling trees.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the pay on that job?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Same, two bits an hour. He was probably working. Well, it wasn't that because we worked, I think it was two and a half a day.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And, we had to work half of the night or sometimes damn near all night to keep the fire to the goal.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: That's what got us disgusted.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, did you have no chance to sleep or anything?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Well, we'd sleep maybe from ten till two or three o 'clock in the morning, three, four, five hours, something like that. Oh, it was rough.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So, you told him to go back to the farm?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Ah, he finally did. Well, we both quit.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: You see, we stayed, as I said, a couple, I don't remember now, but it may be a couple of weeks. Because, you had to give them time to get somebody else if you could. Because, if you wanted to respect the company, you had to kind of give them a little time ahead so you could come back and get a job the next year. That's what I realized.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Well, so I told my brother, now I said we quit here. And I said, you go home. And I said, I had worked, I didn't tell you that. I had worked for the vet, veterinary, potlatch veterinary in old Doc Palmer. Previous. Previous, in the fall, when I first come there. See, before I got into the woods at all. And, so I come back there, I knew him and my aunt, this Ed Swenson's brother's wife, see, he got killed by a tree one time.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: When he was sawing, that was Bombardier, Idaho.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Ed Swenson's brother?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Yeah, Isaac was his name.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And she was living there in Boulder, taking in washing from these lumberjacks.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And I felt sorry for her, and I said, well, you know, I said, if I can board with you, I'll pay you a board, the best I can. And she's just glad to get somebody and make a few dollars. He had a lot of little kids. He had this cousin living in Detroit, now Carl Swenson.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now, you know whether you've met him?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Yes, sure. Yeah. Well, it was him, and then it was, let's see, Ernest and Dolph.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And I guess, well, Oscar, that was her older son. He was working in camp.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: He was old enough to have a job in camp.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: But these other, let's see, it was Carl and Dolph and Ernest, three boys, and a girl named Idie. Bought four of them. She had four yet at home. She was supporting.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you know how it happened that he was killed?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Oh, yeah, I bet. And that got me to lurk.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: When I went to the woods, boy, I become pretty catty about watching trees fall.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was he?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And I, God damn, I didn't tell you that. I'll tell you that accident when I and Simon was gone after a bit.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I'll tell you that. Okay, I'll... Isaac had died.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was he right in Deary when that happened to him?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Oh, yeah. They had a little forty acre place there in Deary then.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was he working for the potlatch or for himself?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Yeah, I was potlatch company up.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And, in a way, he, she, I had to sleep, God, it was cold that winter. All the job I got when I come back to the duck pond was hauling hay and when I got through with that, he said, well, he says, all I got now is snow shoveling for the sleigh hauler.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: See, they'd haul logs on great big bumps just like putting it like a truck. They'd have one team on it and they'd sand the roads so they wouldn't go too fast and going down these mountains, see. And, so, he said, I had asked for a job for teaming but I was glad I didn't get it. Well, they told me I couldn't handle it and I knew I couldn't.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What, a job for what?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: To drive a team on that sleigh haul. But, he gave me this job, a snow shovel, see, after. I stayed right through Clear to Spring.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now, where was this at?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Bovill, right in Bovill.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And, so, in a way, I stayed there and, like I said, it was as cold as 22 below zero and seven foot of snow. Oh, I didn't tell you how we got, how lucky we, my brother and I were that we moved out of Elk River. The snow snowed till it blocked, there was no railroad traffic, no nothing in Elk River.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: It snowed 13 or 14 feet, they said, clear up over to the upstairs windows. My uncle, Adolph Swenson, that picture, I think my cousin still got in, shows a picture of that snow.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Well, we just got out the day before, I think it was a day or so, not over two days after we got out of there till that happened.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So, what happened? Was all the people stuck there?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Oh, God, they were all, they were really sunk till they, no snow plows like they got now. No doggone tractors, nothing.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: They just had to wait till it just, well, I suppose the way they, I don't know how they did to get supplies in, but they had to move and over somewhere. The railroad couldn't get in yet.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Well, probably the railroad did have snow plows on front that they could get out eventually. But God, it took quite a while, you know, that 13 feet of snow dropping down.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Oh, God, it was about four or five feet when we left camp up there. And when we got to, I was more than that because, hell, it was seven feet in Bovill when I got there.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And it stayed, it just kept snowing, kept snowing all winter.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I'll tell you, that year, the snow didn't leave till Owling May.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: In Park.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: God knows when it left in the woods.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Well, listen, when you, now you were working, you were working the snow shoveling you'd done in Bovill.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That was for the sleigh hall?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: That is for the sleigh hall, yeah.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: In 19, that was, well, that would be 1913, see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Can you tell me how your aunt made do without her husband? Did she have any?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Well, she'd know. She had to take in washing. She charged so much of the shirt and ironed them and bended them.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: She was a working hunk. Say, that woman lived to be 97 years old right down here at the home in Lewiston. Oh, she was a husky. But the poor thing, she married again and was married 50 years with her second husband. A fellow named Carl, wait a minute, Carl Carlson. Or Charlie Carlson, they called him. I guess his name was Carl, but they had to change it to Charlie because this boy of hers was named Carl, see.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I think that's the way it was. Well, anyway. So, she did remarry. That probably made it a lot easier. Oh, yeah. He had a little farm on Texas Ridge. So, they had, and he was a carpenter.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Hell of a good carpenter. So, he was making pretty good living for her. That was a relief to her, you know. But then her kids were grown up too by that time, see.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And so, this Carl, down here to Lewiston, I'll say that much for him. He's the only one of them boys that really honored his mother.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: He seemed to it that she had money. After he got big enough, I mean, to work out, he'd always bring her stuff to eat before she married this other man.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And not only that, when they finally sold their farm up there and moved to Lewiston, Charlie, he was a carpenter, of course, and he built a house.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I don't know if he, no, I guess he bought the house, but he remodeled it. Made it handy for her, see. And all. But here's the deal, the way she got hurt.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: She was taking a bath and she sat down on the edge of the tub and slid off and fell and hurt her side. And they had to put her in the house, yeah, in the home.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And there she stayed and stayed and stayed till she finally got so she couldn't realize things. She talked, but she didn't seem to realize hardly what she was saying. Poor girl. And I went down there and visited her several times. Carl always, when she could, understand why he was there and treating her, you know. And always on her birthday, he'd bring her a birthday cake or treat, see. And so did I, I gave her cards and stuff, you know. And she was, well, she had cooked, I didn't tell you that, after mother died. When her and her uncle first moved out from back east, Dad always, my dad was a sympathetic guy.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: He always felt for his brothers. He always helped all his brothers, more or less. And so he felt sorry for them and he hired her to cook one spring over there on the farm. And I guess he had a little work for her husband, too, on the farm. And then to top it all, later on, he got the both of them a job at a mill on Russell Ridge. Ramey Sawmill. Wasn't big money, but it was $60 or $65 a month, but they got bored. The two of them.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And that's how they come to get a little money to buy this 40 -acre tract out by duty. They're going to give $400 for it, see. Had a little log cabin on it. And that's where they were living when uncle got killed. Him and Uncle Gilbert was out in the woods sawing and I could tell you about how that happened.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: It was in the deep snow, see. And he tried to get, they saw this tree, but they hadn't side cut it. Now there's a trick that you've got to know something about when you're falling timber. If a tree's a -leaning, you better side that cut. If it's leaning heavy, especially.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I got so, I was pretty good at it. I cut wood up at Orofino. I mean, made a living cutting wood after I was married. Single -handed by no one thought, see.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: So, I fell in a tree, I'll tell you, show you now. There was a great big, I wouldn't have had to fell, but I just kind of got curious. Bosses said, well, you don't have to take that yellow pine, but if you want to, you can.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Well, this tree I knew damn well I could fall it, because the son of a bitch was just putting air down in the way, leaning, see. Well, that's what I knew better than to try to cut behind. I cut both sides. I just kept a -cutting both sides, see, way in.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Clear over to my undercut.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: You didn't have to have much undercut.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Then I started in over here, and you know what that damn tree broke?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: About that much on the stump. Broke it, because it was leaning so heavy, see. Now, if that, here's what I wanted to illustrate to you. If I'd have done like my uncle and them, they didn't side cut, and that tree split up.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: See what I mean?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Jumped up in the air, because it was on this, it was still fastened to the stump, but she split off, and then she fell over sideways, and my uncle was out here running away from it, and caught him in the side of the head and mashed his head. Killed him deader than a mackerel.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: That's the way he died.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: So what happened was it fell sideways. It fell.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did it split in two?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And he would happen to be right in the way, you see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did it split in two or just fall sideways?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: No! You know what I, here's the idea. Let's see now, I'll get you, I'll illustrate from this flashlight. words: And I've had all them kind of accidents. We had just a state chain and I'd get down on the hound's wagon and beat it, see. And I could make it pull. I did make it pull. I had awful good leaders. That's what helped me. And they'd just claw for dear life anytime I let them have the lines, you know. So I said to myself, boy, you are going to work, boy, golly, find them yourself. speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Well, there was another hill just where you get the warehouse, on the north side of the Clearwater River.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And if the son of a bee didn't start balking him back, right down off the grade towards the Clearwater River.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Man, I wasn't, I bet you I wasn't 30 feet from the north side of that river in how God knows deep.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And I was down on the hound of that wagon, I'd beaten that son of a gun. My leaders were throwing rocks all over me. They tried to hold it, see. They did hold. speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And the warehouseman, they hollered and they said, my God, kid, you come pretty good going in Clearwater, didn't you?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I said, I sure did, but I said, my God, I was going to stay with it as long as I could. We're going to make that son of a bitch pull.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: From then on, that animal pulled for me all right.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How high above the river was it when it went down? How high over, above the river were you when that?
