words: Oh, about 30 feet. That's all that was left until I'd have been down on the back horsing and all. Just to show you what kind of a deal that river was, now, it's ferry boat you had to cross on, see.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: You know what a ferry boat is, and that's not powered by steam, it was powered by the stream.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: They got a cable cross here that holds it, see, and they angle it, so the stream pushes it.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: That's the way it goes cross. I didn't know that. Yeah, that's another little trick that I never forgot.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Well, then one time there happened to be what they call a jerk line skinner that hauled grain from our ridges and different ridges. He'd been an old fazer. He had a big brownie, he called him, that was his jerk line leader. Then he had a fast horse alongside of him with a jockey stick that when he guided it, he'd throw the line on which side to this brownie that was trained in, see. That horse would have to go, he'd have a fast horse, he'd have a hold back trap so he'd only get so far ahead, but he had to stay so far away from brownie.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Well, everything went fine until one day he was home. Oh, God, he had big wagons. One of them, I guess the trail wagon itself was, they called it four inch. Now, three inch is a small wagon, but four inch, that's a heavy something. That was bigger than any one wagon we ever had, but he'd been a big freighter for you in rough country. And he said, if you got a big wagon, he said, no matter how much load you pull on it, he said, well, God, it'll pull easier than a little wagon. We didn't know that, but that's what he claimed.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: But here when he come on this fireboat one day, and he had a big team in the wheel, they weighed about, I guess, a ton of piece. And a swing team, he had six on, see. He was hauling a hundred sacks or better to a load. And God dang the minute he got on this here with all these outfit and the big wagons and all that load, that God damn boat had sprung a leak and down she went.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And the horses went head on into the river.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: But he was quick enough that he got his swing team and leaders back, the wheelers.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: See, by that time the wheelers was shoved in head first down there. He lost the wheel team.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Yeah, I don't know whether he ever got any money out of it or not. I don't think so.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now, what did you have to do to break them off there?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Well, you see, they have a, I don't know how he had it rigged, but generally the way I had it was you'd have a chain from either chain or rod. We used to have a rod back instead of a chain with maybe a hook to the lead boss.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: He'd jerk that hook out of there and then they'd be free. They'd come back, you see. And he had his, lucky he probably wouldn't have done that had he had his horse trained, that leader, that Brownie, you see. He was trained good. He didn't have to do much to him.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: He could just throw that line which way, come on Brownie or something like that, and boy that horse would ride around.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: He knew they were dangerous. He was a good sensible horse. He trained him good.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And so he saved his swing team and Brownie and his partner. It went down that fast. You just put them on the boat and it just went down that fast. Yeah, when the boat started sinking, it was gone. So that's where it's been going and all. And then I got on after I got to farming on Centler Ridge. Now dad, I made pretty good with them hogs. I'd raised hogs for two years. First year, I didn't do so good.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I'd just done enough with one sow and she had, you see, nine pigs.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And so I had her in a lot, but I think it's a quarter or half an acre, that I'd seeded down to oats, green oats and peas, combination for pasture, because we hadn't well felt by that time. Then I had a feeder there that I fed her barley, chopped, rolled barley.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And by golly, in five months, them little devils growed to be around 200 pounds apiece. And that's what started me getting in hogs.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And I took them to Orofino. There's no market in Peck. I had a hog cleared to Orofino, but I got 17 cents a pound.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Well, that made me $34 a hog, see. Well, I got the figuring on that. I said, gee whiz, that's more money than I can raise, get off of raising wheat.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: So the next year, that's when I called on dad to get to move that house for a hog house. And I didn't finish telling you all that. Now all that, every 400 feet, we had to keep digging down three feet in hard pen to pull, to anchor, to pull that house, see.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I just had a coyote team pulling this stump puller, and every 400 feet you had to dig that. In that field I showed you up there, because I wanted to get up on top of that hill. I didn't want to go in the side hill or slide off my skids.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now you put it on skids?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I made them skids out of bull pine logs, peeled them so they'd be slick. And I and a neighbor jacked the house up and slid them under, and then we reinforced it here in front to hook to the pull by, see. And drug that three quarters of a mile through that field, and just as we got it into the hog lot where I was going to put it, or pretty near where I was going to put it, was on some orchard grass.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: The storm broke, and I was carried along in November, see, I was worried about the weather.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I thought, my God, if I could get stuck in the field with that plowed ground, it'd be pretty messy.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: But I happened to make it. Didn't have to, didn't have, I mean I got in on that sod, orchard grass sod, just when it started bad weather.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And I finally moved it over, I told my neighbor, I said, I can finish now, I said I'll move that over there myself. So I didn't have him no more, but I moved it over close to this pond, that's a pond laying there, close to them trees there. I moved it over there and I set it up on foundation high enough that I, three or four feet high, leveled it up, see, and had it high enough so my stock hogs could go underneath. And I could, I had, that was a big enough house that I could have, I had seven sows on one side of Piedgang Way, and seven on the other, fourteen brood sow. And I, they hatched, I took good care of them, I'd learned how to take care of a brood sow, see my brother and I, we took up, we went to the university here and got some agriculture training, two years.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that the short courses that you had?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: That was the short course, yeah.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: It was six month courses, but it done us a lot of good, because we hadn't got, only we didn't get to eighth grade, until we got to go to Spokane College that Dad had took a share in, see, and they didn't charge anyone that bought share in it, wouldn't have to pay a tuition. All we had to pay was our board and room, and that was only four dollars, well there was two rooms, eight dollars a month, and then there's only two ninety a week for board.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: At Spokane. And Spokane College. I'll have to ask you about that later. Yeah. Now tell me about them pigs and that.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Yeah, well anyway, I had awful good luck, that first year especially, I mean that's the second year.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: So I wrote, or I fed them carrots and alfalfa leaves, and boy them little alfalfa grew like everything. They was having Duroc jerseys, see that's a good, Red Hog, they're a hardy hog, and prolific. They had, I had, they averaged, well there's, like I said, I had them fourteen sows, brought me a hundred and nine pigs. And I saved a hundred and five out of that bunch. No kidding.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And so, but I had to borrow money to buy, buy feed because I hadn't thrashed nothing for myself yet.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I went to the bank, I said, Dode, he was, the bank had been broke too and the farmers had been, had organized the bank and they had took it over for, well you might say, they had to reorganize and run it.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: The tax bank, yeah okay.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And Dode Holmes was the president of it at that time. He was one, just a farmer like ourselves, up there on Central Ridge. And so I got to go see him to get by with this money.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What, about what year would this have been?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Well that was in 19, let's see, wait a minute, about 1919. And, wait, no wait a minute, no.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Maybe a little later?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Yeah, later, wait a minute, it was 1923. Okay. The second year. Because the last year I quit, 24. Okay, so it was 23. It was 23, 1923. And by golly, when I told Dode, you know, well thank God, he says, I can't let you have it, he says, I've got to call up the directors and find out, he says, them directors, he says, have got more say really than I have. Well I said, Dode, I'll tell you one thing, I said, I don't want you to take my word.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: You come up and see my hogs so you know what you can tell them, not tell them what I said. And, so he did, he come up the next morning.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And the little devils, it happened to be a nice sunshiney day, and I had a rant, see, the little devils was out here in the sun, sunning themselves, and they'd play around, they'd feel pretty good, see, they was about putting your weaning time, and these little roly poly devils, just fat as a roll of butter. And so when they could smell a stranger, they'd get scared, see, they'd woof woof woof woof woof. They just felled that whole god damn ramp, and the sunshine in the morning looked like gold nuggets flying up there.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: My god, Dode says, woo, he says, that beach, everything, he says, well, he said, you didn't tell no lie. Alright, Elmer, he says, I'll call the bankers, we had a phone them days, I'll call you if it's okay. So he says, the next morning it'll take probably a day or so to find out from the directors. Next morning or the other, a couple of days after that at least, I found out, he called me and said, come down and get your money.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Well, I finished them hogs up with that borrowed money, because I didn't, I told Dode, I said, I'm gonna make a bin on the, Dad had this barn there, you see, and it was 50 foot wide, where that driveway is, and I made a bin all along on that side, and that held 1400 bushels. So that fall I just, I put the grain in the bin.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I didn't sell a darn kernel, I was lucky I did, because the wheat that I had on the fall wheat got smutty, and I wouldn't have got four bits of bushel for the wheat, besides the sacks and the hauling and all, see, all that extra trouble. Of course there was trouble with feeding the hogs, but, that was another little sticker, Dad, he didn't want, first he didn't want to consent to sharing like he did with the grain, that was a third. He wanted half.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Half of the hogs. I didn't know that. I said, now you get a third of the grain, I ate two thirds, but I got to stand at all expenses. Yeah, but he said, you don't have as much expenses. No, but I said, I'll have more work. Well, but he said, you ain't got the sack bill, you ain't got the hauling bill, don't make any difference. I said, if we both make money, and I do all this extra work, I said, this is the only way I'll take it.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: He finally consented. So that's what it was. That way we don't have to keep track of the wheat. Put it in the bin and feed it. Divide the hogs. And by God, he finally consented.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: He finally, see this was after, I'd been there, uh, in 23.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Yeah, I had, still had a lease, you see. Wait a minute, oh, I see, 23, 23, no. That was, my lease was out. I had got it for five years. And, uh, he's the one that coaxed me to stay two more years. I told him I was going to quit. I didn't have to then, my lease was out. And, uh, no, I said, you better stay. He said, well, I'd given him good rent plenty before that, see. Even off of the grain. Because he got a third clear.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: So, yeah, because I didn't tell you that, I'd raid the crop of beans on that particular spot where that flax is raved. I had 44, that was one crop I had that done pretty good. I got, uh, 1300 pounds an acre and, uh, Dad could have got as much money, I mean a third as much money as what I did.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I sold mine for $2100, my two -thirds share. Off that crop of beans.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And then the next year it was, that I got, uh, I was lucky on the wheat, I didn't get a big yield, we didn't fertilize like they do nowadays. We just, uh, and I was greenhorned at starting the farming. I didn't know how to do, like, I learned afterwards. And, and this, this ground of Dad was, had been in clover and it was loose. Instead of letting it stay loose, I should have packed it. And I only got, uh, well that Marcus wheat then was a poor year, 16 bushels.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What year?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Well, that was, that was about, uh, 1900 I think. Wait now, wait now. No, no, it's been later. Maybe 200, 21, something like that.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Nineteen. That was the time when I was born. That's when I had the beans, 1918. Beans was 18, so this would have been 19. This was either 19 or 20. Oh, okay. And, uh, then I got the, and I, I, I was hauling that wheat at Marcus, see. The guy at warehouse money, I was hauling that to this side, that didn't cross the river on that side, that was after they got two, two warehouses, one on each side of the river.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And I didn't want to fool around and go across. Sometimes you, them other guys would get ahead of you and you had to sit there and wait, see.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And they paid practically as much, not quite as much as the other side, but you didn't have to cross that damn ferryboat.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: So I sold to Crawford.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: So, uh, on this side. So you got only a poor yield. You only got 16 bushels of acre. Well, I did, but what I was going to say, I got a big price, see. When I was hauling, I had, I had kept the ground clean because I had plowed my ground in the fall, and then I would disk it in the spring in the harrowed. First I harrowed it to get the fowl stuff going. So it made it awful.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did it get the what stuff growing?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Wild oats and pickle wheat and stuff like that. So when I got two of that, uh, that way, it made a nice clean wheat.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And this market was good, the flower wheat made good flower, see.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And Crawford was interested.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: He said, uh, how much of that you got?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Well, I said, I got a, counting my dad's share, I said, I got a thousand and nineteen sacks.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Ninety, I forgot which it was now.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And he said, uh, well, I said, uh, what do you take for it?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Well, wheat had been going two twelve, two fourteen, sixteen, eighteen, and back down to two ten twelve. So, you jumping around like that.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Pretty soon when he struck me, he says, how would you like two twenty?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: He says, by God, you know, I says, that's all right.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I says, I'll take that or you can give me more if you want to. So, well, he said, that's, that's top price. When you get it all down, he says, you haul it all down. He says, we'll settle for that. I says, okay. So, next day I come down.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Say, he says, how would you like two twenty five?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: He says, I understand the other warehouses are paying more than I offered yet. I said, I don't want to be as good as they are.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Well, hell, I said, you can make it two and a half, you want it. So, I got two and a quarter. See, that's how the price, I mean, that crop yielded pretty good. Sure. In money.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Five thousand one hundred dollars. Dad got seventeen hundred dollars cash.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Besides, I had to pay him three hundred dollars for seed wheat and the hay that I had to feed my outfit for the next year.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: So, that's why he was doing pretty good, don't you see?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And, what about that, that, that was this farm here. Peck, Idaho. Central Ridge. I'm thinking now that the pigs, you didn't really finish telling me what happened with them pigs. Well, that fall, yeah, when I paid, when I, that fall when I sold them, by golly, I didn't get a big price for the hogs. Now, that first fall, that first fall I only got six, six and a half cents.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Two hundred pound, only thirteen dollars for thirteen, I mean, for a two hundred pound hog.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: See, it wasn't one half what I got at Orr Field that time.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: So, shipped them to Spokane, see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, did you sell the whole, whole, whole, whole?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Yeah, I sold them for that, but, and that only made me, well, let's see now, I got thirteen dollars, and, well, I made enough, so I, I, I had a little money left to make, pay my expenses and live on them, and I paid the bank up the five hundred dollars, see.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I had to pay the bank the five hundred dollars.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And, so the next year then, what I was going to say, next year I had all kinds of experience, man on man.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Talk about tough times. My wife got sick of that, see, she brought birth to a dead boy, baby boy, weighed over ten times, ten pounds, see, she went over time. She had what they called, at that time, they called it Bright Disease, I don't know, maybe she had diabetes then. And, this doctor that come and took the baby away from her, she put in her past, she passed out.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And, God, there I was left for them three little kids, Jesus scared the devil out of me. There was three older ones, that's the two girls and the boy, see.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And, he was only, oh, see, that was, what year did I say that?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: It was 19, yeah, that was 1924. And, well now, let's see, the boy would be, he would be six and the other girl would be four. Oh, wait a minute, I got ahead of myself.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: The boy was eight and the other girl was six and four, youngest girl was four.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: They were just kids.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And, but she finally come to, see, God, I was relieved. I sure held my breath for a while, till she come to.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How long was she out there?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Well, she was down, God, that's when I say. That spring I had to tend to them kids, do my own cooking, tend to my horses and some cattle ahead and hogs, sows. God, I got up at three o 'clock in the morning, I had to get up that early to get my work done, get out in the field to do a day's work. Go to bed at nine, ten, eleven o 'clock at night.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: It's all the sleep I'm getting. But I got that fall, I was just a skeleton, believe it or not.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And not only that, I had two mares a -folding and I had this ground that was west of the barn there towards my uncle's place. I had that in the summer fall of that year and had it in the corn and peas that I wanted to fatten these hogs on, see. I did that over that other summer fall or two and I didn't tell you that, I had corn on it. I found out that it was the slickest way, half of it in the corn, half in the peas and they would eat the peas first and then by that time the corn would be ready and then they'd go right into the corn, see. And Dad had his place fenced hog tight. And I was the instigator that talked him in the notion of fencing that hog place hog tight. And he never raised a hog, I mean to make any money, till I got on it.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: That's why he wanted to keep me there, he thought Jesus Christ, I really don't want to tell you though.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And after twenty -four then, when the price come up, I was in debt, forty -four hundred dollars.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Which year, the year that your wife was sick?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Yeah, I was in debt, forty -four hundred dollars and I told her, I said, well Dad, I was having trouble with a neighbor there, his kids had been picking on my boy and I wanted to get the hell out of there. And I said to Dad, I said I want to quit. I said I'll sell out everything to you, I said for what I owe you, I'll sell out everything to you. I allowed him two thousand dollars for somebody down here, for his outfit at first and that was what I went into debt for.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Allowed him two thousand, see. Well, and then equipment and that is for everything.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I went in debt to him, listen, and then I still owed him eleven hundred and sixty dollars. And I wanted to wipe the slate clean if he'd take my outfit, including two colts and two extra horses, oh, see with him. No, it was two colts and the five horses I bought of him, see I had seven, I was farming with seven heads. Five heads that I bought of him and a brand, not a new binder, but an eight foot binder and another drill, his drill and binder wasn't to suit me.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And a chop mill cost a hundred dollars and no, he couldn't see it.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I knew I'd have a problem, you know, dealing with him. Well, I said Dad, I'll tell you what I'll do, he wanted me to sell out.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: But first he said, why don't you stay? No, I said Dad, I'm going, I said I've got the hunch to go and I said, why don't I go?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Because I worked like a damn slave all summer and I said I ain't going to take it no more. He said, by God, I want to get out of here.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I said, no, I'm in deep debt, and I said, by selling the hogs now?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: That's what I didn't tell you. The hogs went up, you see you had to sell half of them before you did the other half because that's the way they fattened out.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And I got eight and a half cents for them and the other's ten and a half and the wheat jumped up to a dollar forty -five or fifty. I said, by doing all the selling now, I'll clean you and the bank and I'll have a little to live on.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And I'll have a team, oh yes, there's another thing I forgot to tell you.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: The bank is, God damn that's what raised hell with me. It got us interested in some of these Guernsey cows. We bought Guernsey in there and I had four heads to move with. That is two heifer calves and two cows to move to park with. And I figured I could go over there and there was plenty of pasture and go into cattle and raise a little hay, see.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Take it more easy and go out to work in between.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Which I did, but what I couldn't say, the old man he resented, oh he didn't want to take it. But I said, finally I said, listen, you mark down everything you've paid for the so and so horse and I said I'll mark it.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I wanted to put it on two sheets so there wouldn't be no misunderstanding because he tried to jume out of it, see.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Which we did. And he shook his head and hell it come to fourteen hundred dollars right ahead of him. He had put his own price on. I told him eleven hundred sixty what I would do, I wanted this square with him.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: But when we got two figured up, it come to fourteen hundred dollars.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: His own price and he sat and he didn't allow me near what the horse, I said God you ain't paying more than about half what them horses cost me. Yeah but they're older, well alright I said. Well your sheet, you made up your own sheet and you had a working horse. And the same, we both marked what he agreed to pay. I see. And when he sold out, wait now. So help me, he had kids to help him for him see.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And he had a damn kid cuddin' and some other kids, hoe and beans I guess. God damn they were sleeping up in that barn and burnt the god damn barn down, burnt his hay and harness and every god damn thing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did he do that?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Well the kids was up there sleeping and they struck a match to try to find some candy mind you, the damn little brats. So then that broke his heart right so he wanted to quit himself then.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That's when, that was after you?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Oh that was after I moved out, yeah. You see we squared up.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh square up now, did he take your outfit then?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Oh yeah he took that. He knew he was getting a good deal then. There but then when he sold, I don't know if I can tell you there. When he sold out I told him, I said Dad you can always sell for better money than I can.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Because he had been an insurance agent in that country, clear up around this place. I said you got a lot of old timers that'll come and bid good on your safe. Being that you've had this hard luck. Which they did.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: By God people come from all over, up around where he'd been canvassing, selling this fire. He started that farmers mutual fire insurance company and they all flocked in there and God they just gobbled up his stuff like nothing. And paid big price, he got double what I got for it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well this is because did he go around and tell them he was going to have the sale?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: No they could phone around them basically. Or they had newspaper, I don't know how he did it. But they all come because he was well known in the country. Oh yeah because he had old friends everywhere. They felt bad about the fire. Yeah and they felt bad for him for that fire. Well I did buy, just to show you, I did, he had a little old bottle of tea, car. Tury.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: 19, what was that?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Oh about a 19, 12, 15 something like that. I bought that in 55 dollars.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: It's the only thing I bought of him. Of course I had to pay him later, I didn't want to dig up, maybe he took it out of what I had coming. You see I had some coming.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How many years after you sold him did this happen? Was this many years later?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: No it wasn't too many.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Let's see, I guess that would be about, right now, 26, 27.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: No yeah here I'm getting a little far ahead. After the year or two years, 24, 25, 25 I worked out in the farming country here hauling grain.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I bought a, made a down payment on a Model T truck, ton truck. So I went out and picked up sacks after a combine. She made a little money that way.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: But I lost it because I couldn't make the payments and I just let them take it. So after that I just went out and sown sacks in the harvest. But anyway. What I want to know is that before we get ahead of yourself, you're saying that I want to know about the year that you sold out to your... Well that's 24. That was 24. And I think he, then the 26 and 27 was the wet years.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: See I would have got sunk. He lost grain. That's what made him quit. He didn't want to lose his barn.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: But he lost grain. The wet years rotted the shocks. I worked on combines down here by Pullman. By God when it was damn wet, we worked 21 days and it started raining.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Got some muddy, see. And the wheat got so soft.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And so anyway, he finally quit then and he sold, he gave his place away. He finally sold that place for $10 ,000. The guy that bought it had a pretty good back and he had a half a... Yeah he had a half a... No.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: He had a quarter section of land before that. But he was in the clear and he had some folks, I guess his mother had a little money and they had enough to start buying it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well in 24 when you quit, did you get out of debt entirely when you sold it?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: That's when I'm going to tell you how hard up I got. I come over here to park and dad was, he resented me enough.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: He owned that granddad place but he wouldn't let me rent there, I mean live there. But I played smart. I had cash rent, cash money so I went and rented a fellow by the name of Corn Place. For $100 a year, the whole place, that's all I paid him.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And I moved my cattle in there and they had a good barn there, good log barn, good house, just as good as dad's on the homestead. Now wait, now before you tell me this, now you're saying the cattle, you had said before... Well I got these cattle.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah, now did them cattle put you in debt?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: They sure did, I kept staying there, I was going to tell you that. I kept staying there, see, too long. I come there in 24.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And I stayed on this corn place till 28 and I got sick and tired of paying that $100 a month, or a year.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Because boy it was, well here, the cream prices went down so low.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: You know what it got so that I could only get a dollar and a half for a five gallon can of cream?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And usually I couldn't raise enough hay on that little shirttail farm to feed them.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: So there was a doctor in there, had 90 acres of good land, pretty level land, that I rented for to cut...

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: What they call bull pine trees for the rent. So I'd get the whole crop myself. It was a, and the only thing, they didn't have cattle water there, only a certain time of the year. And when it got dry summer I'd either haul water or drive them three miles to a pond. I got so I had to haul with a tank for our, yeah for wash water. There wasn't drinking water down in the draw there.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Now this other place had a good spring on it, see, but, and it really was the place, but it just didn't have ground enough.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It had a, it was a hundred dollars, a hundred dollars, was that it?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Yeah, a hundred dollars a year for the whole place, I'd get what I could raise on it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So you were getting worse and worse off this time when it went on there?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Oh, I stayed there, you see, in part, on this doctor's place from 28 to 32. And I had a hell of a bunch of cattle. I'd been, well, one year I bought some state land, see, that was for dad, I mean before I rented over there, and dad, he didn't think I'd done a wise trick, which I didn't, but I didn't know it. I wanted some land, and there was 80 acres laying up and down alongside of one of these places that I rented, and it was state land. And I bought that for, see, 10 % down, $1400 for the 240's.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And I had to pay down $400 cash, see, I borrowed that from my brother Simon, that's, died when he was 64.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I paid him back though, and, yeah, I paid him back when I was farming over there, he was paid back when I was farming on the big place. But anyway, I stayed there on that place, and I tried to log that place, and could have got it logged if it hadn't, if snow hadn't have melted, see.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I bought a team, heavy team, and went in debt on that, and I bought a wagon, team and wagon, and a bump sled, and heavier harness, and I had to borrow $1000 in the dairy bank to do this.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: So then,

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: the snow, the snow left, you see, and I couldn't, I got a soil man to help me soil this big tree, big nice tree on Dr. Spree. He had consented to me at logging it, and he would have got his share out of that, to only just pay me for logging it, see.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And I would log my own, that is that state land, I had a few thousand on it, so I'd pay it. No snow, this one, no snow next window, no snow, no snow, I'd just give it up. Well then I stayed there from 28 to 32, and the price of weird loats and everything went down, down, down.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And Cal, you couldn't give that bunch of dairy stuff away, I really was broke that time. If I hadn't had them god damn things, I could have been working out, and done a lot better, and I told the mister, for god's sake, let's quit.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I says, the bank, I hate to quit, but I says, the bankers loaned me that money, I still own the $1000, but I says, well, they got the mortgage on the cattle, I says, I'll just let them take them for the $1000. Now if it had been prices like what I give, $125 for one of them cows, when I bought. And then I couldn't sell them at all. That's how bad the times were. Now some of these times, some of these kids around think, by god, they're having a tough time now. By god, they ought to have had what I had. And they'd think different. Oh, I got to tell you a little incident about it, a little accident I should say.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: My uncle, Ed Swenson, was with me, he'd been in the park there, he used to come in there, he lived at Dearie then. He used to come in there and do a little hunting, and him and his brother Adolph and I. So I was taking these cream cans out to Dearie in a bobsled, and I also had a buggy in there, because when I got to the highway, I had to take that buggy out and drive on the highway four miles.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I had this cow use team with a couple of brown gildings on there, and we got up the hill going, we had two cans of cream in there like I said.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And it wasn't much, I didn't get much out of them, but well that time I think they were about $3 a can maybe, a $5 can. Well, God damn this one wild Montana cow he said I had, he got a bit out of his mouth. And he got scared when he could see back, and he started to gallop and run away. And I got scared, he got me shit up my neck, I jumped out the back of the sled with a hay rack on it, I said, Ed, I said, get out of there, let them go! You get hurt! I said, you get hurt, you know, you're stumped on the side of the road, crazy horse is running away, let them go! And I said, for God's sake, no, he stayed and held them.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: God damn, he stayed right with them, it was just luck and havoc. There were some woodcutters in the road, damn pretty, by that time the horses had just about threw runs in there, it was all winded, see, and they come out in the road, they see there's a runaway, they stopped them! And I had to walk about a mile, a mile and a half to catch up.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: That spilt the cream, didn't have it wired on, they fell down, and maybe, oh, enough for a little coffee or something.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: See, Ed was busy holding the horses, the cream ran out while he was having trouble.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Well, we got to Deary, er, to Helmer then, and loaded the buggy out, and I guess we must have took, took it all in.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Well, Ed had to go home, I think that's the way it was, I took him in, you see, he wanted to get home. And, er, well then another time, when I was, that, I'll tell you that story too, that's a true, it's all true stories.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: So then, er, that's when I was living on this doctor's place, selling this cream. In the summertime, we had a neighbor that we always exchanged work with, wrists. And they had a pick -up, and I didn't, and I was, like I said, I was working out in the harvest field a lot of times, and we made the agreement that if she would haul, she was the one that drove it, if she would haul my cream in the summer, I would haul her cream in the winter. So, er, she would take that pick -up, see, and haul her cream when I was out to work. That way it got, got to market. Well then, by golly, wait now, yeah, one winner there, it's no, God, it's no, that's too, poor feature.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And boy, you couldn't go, one team couldn't go through that with any kind of a load of talk.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Well I, lucky enough, I had a friend, this poor guy now, he just died here last week, Charlie, Charlie Jelliberg, I talked to him. Charlie Jelliberg, he had a team, and, er, Earl Austin had a team, and I had four, we put eight horses on, and I made a double, I mean a collapsible snow plow out of planks. I just got that idea and figured that we, we could snow plow, we got to the bottom of Potlatch, and then when we get there we just take them out and it'd just be the empty planks for the horses to pull back, because they'd be tired.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: By God, the boys, they agreed. And but hell, the other side wasn't plowed nothing. Next morning I had to take that seventeen cans of cream out to Deary with that Cayuse team and that snow to foot or two that night.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I had to break that road down to Potlatch that much, and then I had to go up and break my own road, and I would go dressed the way to the highway with that one Cayuse team, I'd take the doubletrees off, see. I learnt that when I was working for Doc Palmer at Potlatch, he told me in that deep snow I had to just break a road with a team and a doubletree. And that was off to the haystack, see, off the main road.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You mean you broke the road just by using the doubletrees?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Yeah, well, enough so the horses could get through.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Doing that snow season, yeah, I remember that. You broke that, now when you did that, explain to me again how you did that. You used a doubletree and the horses just enough to... Yeah, I went up the hill, see.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Two or three times you had to go with that deep snow, don't you see?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Pack it down.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And then I'd pull them a load, fourteen cans of cream, as far as they could go, and puff them, and go a little ways and puff them, and then when I'd get to as far as I broke the road, then I'd have to unhook again and go on.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Well, what I was going to say, it took me from six o 'clock in the morning to get to Helmer. I had to stay at Helmer all night.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: You know where the Helmer is. An old Carl Lancaster, his dad -in -law had a Wilkins. Wilkins, sure.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Yeah, they had a barn, log barn. I asked them if I could stay all night or so, I'll pay for it. Well, that's all right, they said, and he had a store there, too, see, and he said they had hay.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I had my own grain, and next morning, you know, I wanted to pay, and they wouldn't even take us in. There's a good friend of mine, that's Carl, the Rancaster.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: We'd meet each other and have good old time talks once in a while, you know. I know him. I like him a lot.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you say puff them, what do you mean puff, puff them?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Well, that means resting them. Yeah.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Did that go in there? What? That's my talk now?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, yeah, yeah. I'll take that. Hey, now listen, don't let it stop you from talking. I'm just the same as we were talking before. Well, that's the way it went, and let's see.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: You know, it's like I said, I got there, it took me twelve hours, six o 'clock until six o 'clock.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about getting back? Was that okay then?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: No, I finally, the next day, Uncle Ed lived in Deary then, and so I made it a point to go there the next day with my team and buggy.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: See, I had the buggy then there, already there.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And so, you see, I parked it there at Wilkins's from the time before.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And so then I stayed all day the next day with Uncle to rest my horses.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: My God, they'd had a hard day of it the day before, see. I wasn't about to kill them entirely.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: They done awful good to plow that road. There was about two miles that I broke all by myself.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You see?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Uphill business put near me.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Well, not quite, but there's a lot of it anyway. That grade, you know how long that is. Well, that wasn't this grade, that was the old grade. It was worse than this one. Yeah, that was the old grade, so that was quite a bit different. That was the other, that was across the road. That was before the potlatch got the road in there, you see.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And, anyway, I got through it and so I just rested. The next, I guess I won't tell that, maybe it's a doll. It's one neighbor that never used to haul, exchange, hauling cream.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And he called up Ed Swenson to see how I made it. And, of course, I liked the monkey. I told him, fine. I said, it's good now. I said, get to get him to come out, see. And he come out the next day and it took him till two o 'clock to get to Deary. And I met him on the street and he says, I thought you said it was a good road.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Well, I said, I figured it was a lot better than when I went. I had to break it all by myself.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I said, you ought to be ashamed of yourself, as if being an old timer in there.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: You wouldn't even as much as offer to haul my cream can, let alone any other combination. And I said, boy golly, I said, the wrist always did.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I said, there was a real neighbor. We neigbored back and forth like neighbors should.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And, well, he said, it sure took a long time to get through there. Well, I said, that's good for you.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you say two o 'clock, you mean two in the morning?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Well, he was lucky to get there. He had a good fresh tea, of course.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And my horses and the sled had broke a road. It hadn't snowed anymore. He could get through it pretty good. He might have to rest his horse a little bit when going up the grade. But other than that, he could plug along. He couldn't go fast, but he could.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So, when he got in there at two in the afternoon, what time would you get in there if things were just smooth? If things were smooth, what time would you get in there?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Oh, I could make that and bought, well, General Ruh.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Let's see. I'd go over, let's see, about, oh, not over two hours.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Whew! Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, you tell me, just how important was that neighboring? Huh?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How important was that neighboring when you were in Park? Did people neighbor really close?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Well, the thing was, us folks at Rade had cream to sell, see the idea. Especially when I went out to work in the harvest field. Then it was very important for me to see that my cream got out. My wife couldn't drive. She had little kids to take care of in the garden. So, with Mrs. Ritz, she was a nice neighbor lady there.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: She lived close by there. She'd always drive in, pick up our cans, take them out. Well, that's the way that went, oh, I tell you. Well, then, now here's some more if you want to hear the rest of it. Sure do. Well,

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: in 1932, as I said, I told the bank, I made the agreement with the bank that they was to take my outfit for the mortgage. And I was going to have a clean slate.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And I got into it, my dad and my uncle, Ado, was helping me to move out.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I had a little what little I could haul out on this car. No, it was a Ford truck, that's right, a tongue truck. And I got down, oh, I didn't tell you, I made an arrangement with Angelie, lived out here southeast of Moscow, to live on his place for the rent, do his chores for the rent, so I could get my kids to school.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And so then, of course, when we got down to Troy with my load coming in to get over to this place, this Angelie had put in, yeah, I'd already unloaded one load. But I didn't tell you that I'd brought one load first, some fruit and stuff. And I come in, well, here he had another family in there. Man, was I mad. And Dad says, oh, you better turn back. No, Dad, I says, I'm going, so I'll make that devil cough up. And I was awful mad because of that. And anyway, I told him, I said, Uncle, I said, well, let's go into Moscow and see if we can find him. So we found out he was in the pastime in Moscow there. Well, I says, Adolph, call him out. I says, I can't go in there, I'd fight him right in there. I said, you better call him out. All right, he called him out, didn't he come out there. And I said, you done me one dirty trick, I said. Promised me that place to do the chores, and I said, you moved another family. And after I had possession, if I'd been smart, I could have sued him, you know. I was smart enough to do it. Dad didn't think of it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: We could have had one, but I didn't want to be in that kind of a pickle, don't you see?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I had a family there moving. I had to get someplace. So, well, I said, it's going to cost you plenty, buddy. We're going to stay right here until you fix up something. Well, he was pretty slick. He found this neighbor, friend of his, I should say, mix, GovMix. He says, I'll call him up, see if I can, he got a house back in the backfield. He said, he might let you have and get some work from him. Fine, I said, good, well. Oh, yeah, and he had offered me $25, you know, for damages, but I found out afterwards, when he got me that, I was big -hearted and give it back to him. And I found out I wouldn't have got that cashed anyway, because his credit was no good.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: There's checks.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: So I got a, I moved the family out there to mix this place, about three miles out in the country, in the dirt road.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And my kids, these older girls, they were, let's see, how old was they then?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Twenty, thirty -two, wait a minute, thirty -two. This youngest one, she was seven, you see, she was born in twenty -five. And eighteen, see, the oldest one was fourteen, I guess.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: You know, eighteen, twelve, yeah.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And the other one would be twelve, twelve, fourteen, and five. And the boy, he would be sixteen.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Anyway, they had to walk to school all that distance, in that dirt road.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Stormy old man. And I had to get up and milk cows for that bugger, five o 'clock in the morning, and then fix fence during the day. Little old dollar a day. Bored myself, built my family on that dollar. Well, he wasn't going to charge me no rent, to start with, until I got it.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Them days, that was the year that old Roosevelt had the WPA. And it was a rock crusher, and Moscow wasn't all rock streets. Down here on Lewis Street, they had to have four horses on a wagon, just a little bunch of rock in the bed of the wagon would sink down halfway. It happened to be, what year, see, thirty -four that year that I got that job. And it was running down, I mean the wagon would sink down halfway on the spokes.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And I was out there pounding with a sixteen pound ball, what they call a nigger head, to get them through the rock crusher. And, forty dollars a month, that's all you got. But you wasn't starving on it, you could get by. And I got this house rented after I quit that son of a gun.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: See, I got a house on Polk Street for ten dollars a month.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And they'd had a big fireplace in the basement.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: So, you said he didn't charge you at first, then he didn't charge you at the end.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Yeah, yeah, and then when I quit, I had money coming from him, but he held that out for rent. He charged me seven dollars and a half a month rent. So it absorbed all I had coming, see, all he was just slicker. So I got stung that way anyway, but I made it. I got over to, got moved into, oh yeah, here was the deal. His pal was a McLean, he was a bookkeeper there in this place, see. And, so he wouldn't put me on right away, but one day after I got moved, yeah, he said, you're farming out there, you're on a farm. So I'm not farming, I'm just living there, working for Micks. They couldn't put me on, he said, on that account. Now they were cronies, see. But old Harvey Smith, he was a civil engineer, he was a boss of the whole works. And I come in there one day and I was telling them about this house that I had rented right here in town. I had moved in town and I wanted to get on. No excuse, I couldn't after I got in town. Because out there we had to haul water even. No water, there was a well that was there, was dead rabbits in it. Couldn't use it even for wash water until I cleaned it out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where'd you haul that water from?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: The city. I had to haul it from the city.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You made a buck a day working for him, but you still used to get by?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Yeah.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Oh, well I had a little, you see. Roosevelt made it stipulation that any crop you raised when you had a family on 55 acres you could, regardless of mortgage, that's how I had a little money to move on. I took that bunch of oats that I sold, see, I pocketed every load in my pocket.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And the banker never said a word.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now this was, you could grow a crop on that guy's place?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Oh yeah, that is not, that, I'm getting probably too far ahead here. This was in Park, you see, on the doctor's place. Oh, oh, in that place.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: But I'm trying to tell you that I was allowed that much money, see.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I mean, that much crop.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So you had some money to live on when you did here?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Yeah, oh yeah.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Oh God yeah, you bet, I wouldn't have moved it. No, no, no, no. And so, then I got this other job and...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You said you, you told Harvey Smith that you wanted to work?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Yeah, Harvey Smith, he told this fellow McLean, he says, Ring said you got children going to school. I said, yes sir. Well, he says, McLean, he says, put Ring Sage on. He's got children going to school. He's got a right to have a job.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: So I got on. There you go then. And I stayed on that winter.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And I guess, yeah, that was the summer, and the next summer I got a job down at... Oh, wait a minute, I'll tell you pretty soon.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Laird Park. You know, that's down towards Potlatch. I and a guy here in Moscow, we applied for work through the WTAAC.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And, no, that was for the Forest Rangers, Forest Service. And got down there and quite a few bunch of, we used to clean that park up see.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And...

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: So then, nobody seemed to be able to file a saw, cross cut saw. And gauge it, you know, and I also had practice enough to learn to file them good.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Make them cut good.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: So they wanted to know if anybody could do this kind of work. I stuck my neck out, told them I could. They said, you're gonna, that's your job, you gotta file these saws. Well, that's what I did down there that summer. Well then, when I got to with that, along in the fall, I had this old model P I was telling you about. I drove clear to Wenatchee to pick apples for three cents a box.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know where Wenatchee is?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I went down there and picked apples for three cents a box, started to. And I knew I couldn't make much that way. So, here come, I was in the past time, and a fella... Had a sign up on the Blackboard, you know, Teamster wanted it.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: He was getting logs.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Well, gee, I knew all about that. I'd already learnt that up in the woods. And it was five dollars a day and bored. Boy, that was more than I could make of picking apples. I found out where this guy was, and he come in there and I got the job. And it was a heck of a place. It was a mountain, there was shale rock, and the great big yellow pine trees. And boy, you had to know your stuff, and try to figure out how to get them logs out of there without too much work.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: So, I had another partner along with me, another team.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And he says, how in the world are you going to get them logs out of that side hill without rolling down the hill?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I said, listen buddy, hook your team, your tongs on the back end of that log, and I says, I'll have mine in front here. And I said, you be sure to have them socked in good and tightened up. So, when I go, we both go, and don't give up, keep coming, until we get out to the main trail down the hill.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: He says, why?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I said, boy golly, I know, if you don't, that will go down the hill.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: It will roll down the hill. Well, it worked.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was he breaking, or was he just following you?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: No, he just followed you. It was a pretty big log. The two couldn't hardly pull it anyway. It went right along like that. I got out, and so I made a little, well I got by, I got the five dollars a day, but it didn't last too long.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where was this place at anyway?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: This was Wenatchee. Yeah, when I was picking apples.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Wait, tell me one thing here, about what was it like there, and the apple picking at Wenatchee? Was there lots of people there picking?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Oh yeah, they come from all over, all kinds of people. Yeah, you bet. But, then later years, after that, then I had learned a little how to pick, and cut it in mind, this Ervin Swenson, he wanted to go with me. That was 1928, the year that I got through up here, no wait a minute, that was 1928, now wait a minute to see.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Well, that's when I lived in Park, we done that, that's right.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: We got down there, we got a little more then, though we got, I think it was about five cents a box we got.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: So we made a little bit better, but you had to board yourself, they had the cabins, see.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: You boarded yourself, you paid ten cents a day for, they had a cook stove, and slab wood, you burnt, you had to do your own cooking.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you get your own cabin, or did you share a little piece?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Well, they rented, the orchard had the cabins all over, see, for the pickers.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How many people would live in one cabin?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Oh, it would be different, different orchard had different sized bunches to pick, see. That's what I do. Now this, down there a few years back, now they're doing different, they got, instead of these little hand boxes, they got these great, but you better shut that thing off. Well, I was thinking, I was thinking I should get going and come back another time. Yeah, I believe so.
